text: The video shows a 5.5 cm tumor located at the left lower pole of the kidney, primarily lateral with some posterior orientation.
The surgeon observes a deep tumor extending into the renal sinus, indicating its significant depth and involvement with the kidney's internal structures.
The surgical team prepares to perform a retroperitoneal nephrectomy using the DaVinci SI robotic system.
The surgeon uses a balloon dilator to create space in the retroperitoneal area before inserting the ports, with the balloon visibly expanding as it accepts air.
The surgeon insufflates the retroperitoneal space, expanding it to facilitate access for the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon places a surgical instrument through an incision in the mid axillary line, located just above the iliac crest.

The surgeon adjusts the port placement lower in the retroperitoneum using the DaVinci SI robot to access a large lower pole tumor. A balloon dilator is used to expand the retroperitoneal space for better instrument maneuverability.
The surgeon inserts the camera port into the dilated retroperitoneal space, preparing for visualization of the surgical area.
The surgeon uses a Hassan type cannula equipped with an internal balloon, visible within the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon inserts the first posterior port underneath the twelfth rib using a zero-degree scope, following the placement of the camera port.
The surgeon identifies the erector spinae muscle and the twelfth rib, positioning the eight millimeter robotic posterior port in the angle between these anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic instrument through the posterior port to push the peritoneum off anteriorly, creating space for the placement of two additional robotic ports.
The surgeon utilizes a four-arm robotic approach, positioning the robotic arms to effectively retract the kidney during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon arranges the robotic arms, positioning the number three arm, number two arm, camera, and number one arm in sequence for the nephrectomy procedure.
The assistant operates a twelve millimeter port with insufflation, positioned near the anterior suprailiac spine.

The surgeon ensures all robotic ports are placed under direct vision, with the peritoneum pushed off anteriorly to create space. The port configuration is complete, and the robotic system is prepared for docking to begin the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins the procedure by entering a small surgical space, using techniques to push surrounding tissues away to create more working room.
The surgeon identifies the posterior body wall, the psoas muscle, and the quadratus lumborum muscle. The surgeon begins by pushing the gyrotus fascia away from the scope.
The surgeon identifies the gyrotus fascia and pushes it away to create more space in the surgical field. The scope is oriented to facilitate this maneuver.
The surgeon rotates the scope ninety degrees to orient the view transversely across the psoas muscle, establishing it as the first anatomical landmark in the surgical field.
The surgeon rotates the scope to position the psoas muscle transversely across the screen as a landmark. The surgeon then enters Girota's fascia, revealing the space where the kidney is located.
The surgeon makes an incision in Girota's fascia, focusing on the top edge, which is crucial for later identification of the kidney.
The surgeon continues the incision in Girota's fascia parallel to the psoas muscle, revealing perinephric fat surrounding the kidney.
The surgeon continues to dissect along the psoas muscle towards the hilum, revealing the kidney as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robotic system to hold the kidney back while continuing to open Girota's fascia, revealing more of the kidney and surrounding structures.

The surgeon is inside Girota's fascia, advancing towards the hilum while working down towards the ureter. The surgical field shows the kidney surrounded by perinephric fat, with the ureter becoming visible as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon identifies the gonadal vessels and ureter within the lower retroperitoneum, clearly visualizing these structures before proceeding towards the renal hilum.

The surgeon exposes the renal artery using the retroperitoneal approach, with the artery clearly visible within minutes of dissection. This approach allows direct access to the artery as the first vessel encountered, unlike the transperitoneal approach where the vein must be navigated first.
The surgeon uses the retroperitoneal approach to expose the artery first, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon positions the fourth robotic arm to enhance retraction of the kidney, improving visibility of the artery within the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon uses two robotic arms to dissect the hilum in the retroperitoneal space, enhancing visibility and control compared to one-handed laparoscopic surgery.
The surgeon identifies the renal artery, noting the presence of additional arteries, including a lower pole vessel. The visual focus shifts to the vein, prompting further examination.
The surgeon warns the assistant about limited suction due to standard insufflation, while the renal vein is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the artery, considering the possibility of addressing the vein due to the central location of the tumor.

The surgeon identifies an additional artery branch near the vein, referencing a complex vasculature model. The anatomical structures are visualized in the surgical field, highlighting the intricate arrangement of veins and arteries.
The surgeon exposes the vein, providing a clear view of the surrounding anatomical structures. The surgical field reveals detailed vasculature, aided by a 3D model for reference.
The surgical team has exposed the vein, which is visible in the operative field, indicating readiness for potential use during the nephrectomy procedure.

The surgeon examines an artery branch, potentially a branch off the main artery, while identifying another vessel nearby. The surgeon inspects additional vessels, considering the possibility of a ureter among them.
The surgeon explores the lower pole of the kidney, searching for a vessel identified in imaging. The ureter is visible and identified during the exploration.
The surgeon identifies a large vein within the surgical field, likely one of the major veins involved in the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the cut edge of Girota's fascia at the renal hilum and begins to pull it up, working within this layer to locate the tumor.
The surgeon addresses the perinephric fat hanging outside Girota's fascia, manipulating the adipose tissue surrounding the kidney.

The surgeon highlights the cut edge of Girota's fascia, emphasizing the importance of staying inside this layer to avoid entering the peritoneum. The tumor is visually identified as a large mass encompassing the lower posterior portion of the kidney, with a significant venous branch visible at its base.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to assist in the dissection, applying downward pressure on the tumor to facilitate working above it. The fourth arm is positioned to enhance stability and control during the retroperitoneal approach.
The surgeon transitions from dissecting normal kidney tissue to approaching the tumor, using the normal kidney as a landmark for orientation.

The surgeon encounters sticky fat during the removal process from the kidney capsule, carefully working about a millimeter above the kidney to avoid subcapsular dissection. The large tumor encompasses almost the entire kidney, indicating minimal remaining kidney tissue post-procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the lower pole tumor, ensuring the ureter is identified early to avoid accidental damage. The tumor is visible, and the surgeon navigates around the bottom of the kidney, noting the presence of a vessel encountered during the procedure.
The surgeon prepares to use an endoscopic ultrasound to identify the tumor on the renal surface, aiming to assess the depth of the tumor and plan the margin for resection.
The surgeon identifies a large vessel within the perinephric fat, noting its neovascularity and potential connection to the tumor. The vessel is visible amidst the surrounding adipose tissue.

The surgeon applies a hemo lock to a vessel, discussing the use of ICG to visualize perfusion areas. The surgical team considers switching instruments to push the kidney away, indicating manipulation of the kidney for better access.

The surgeon uses ultrasound imaging to mark the margin of resection for a large tumor. The ultrasound reveals a projection of the tumor into the sinus fat, confirming observations from previous imaging.

The surgeon uses an ultrasound probe to visualize the tumor and its projection into surrounding tissue. The Doppler function is used to differentiate between the tumor and vessels. The surgeon marks the tumor edge and adjusts the probe angle to better view the tumor's boundary against the normal kidney tissue.
The surgeon manipulates the tumor, demonstrating its boundaries by moving on and off the tumor mass, indicating the transition between tumor presence and absence in the surgical field.
The surgeon examines the base of the tumor using high-quality ultrasound imaging, revealing extensive tumor presence.
The surgeon observes numerous vessels at the base of the tumor, noting a significant cut. The protrusion is visible at the midpoint of the tumor, extending deeper into the sinus.

The surgeon prepares for a nucleation of the tumor, marking an incision that includes a small portion of cortical tissue above the tumor. The deep portion of the tumor is visible, and the surgeon plans to quickly locate the tumor during the procedure.

The surgeon exchanges two instruments, switching the retracting arm to the one closest to the camera, identified as the number two arm. The surgeon adjusts the positioning to improve exposure and facilitate movement, ensuring the assistant can effectively assist during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies one artery and a large vein, likely a lumbar vein, in the surgical field. The decision is made to take the vessel to improve access, guided by imaging results.

The surgeon uses suction to clear the surgical field, revealing the anatomical structures beneath. The camera focuses on the area of interest as the surgeon prepares to identify the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon uses suction to clear smoke from the surgical field, revealing another vein in the area.
The surgeon identifies and points out the lumbar vein, noting its position as it extends back. The gonadal vein is also identified, located higher up in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and the gonadal vein, tracing the gonadal vein upwards to improve visibility of the adjacent vessel.

The surgeon identifies the aorta and examines the surrounding area, noting the presence of a lumbar clip. The dissection reveals a segmental artery making a turn, with the surgeon considering its unusual direction as they continue to explore the anatomical structures.

The surgeon traces a vessel that curls in front of the main renal vein, identifying it as a segmental artery originating from the main renal artery. The vessel's position suggests it could be the splenic vein or possibly the inferior mesenteric vein. The ureter is visible nearby.
The surgeon prepares to clamp vessels using three bulldog clamps and a bolster, ensuring no harm regardless of the vessel identity. The surgical team positions a bag for specimen collection.

The surgeon prepares the extraction sack and begins cutting to bag the specimen. Needle drivers are used, followed by a long four-oh stitch to secure the area. A vessel is identified, but its exact nature is uncertain.
The surgeon instructs the assistant to push into the surgical field, focusing on the area without the vein first. The pneumoperitoneum is adjusted to enhance visibility.
The surgeon performs a segmental excision, cutting through normal tissue rather than enucleating, as visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies and manipulates the adipose tissue surrounding the renal collection system, visible from the inside of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a metal five-millimeter clip on a large vein, preparing to cut across it with plans to suture it later.
The surgeon transitions from cutting through the renal cortex to performing blunt dissection as they approach the renal sinus, focusing on the collecting system.
The surgeon identifies a tumor and clips a large vessel using a surgical clip, preparing to address the vessel during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon applies a clip to a vessel, ensuring good access and positioning. Suction is used to clear the area, followed by the application of another clip.
The surgeon prepares to suture in the lower retroperitoneum using needle drivers and a 4-0 suture, focusing on the large vessels within the collecting system.
The surgeon requests suction in the surgical field while preparing to place another stitch using a needle, coordinating with the assistant to bring the needle closer.
The surgeon identifies another calyx and uses suction to clear the area, revealing the anatomical structure within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a large vein in the surgical field and instructs the assistant to secure it, highlighting its significance in the procedure.
The surgeon uses an absorbable monofilament suture to re-approximate the medullary tissues over the renal sinus, aiming to close the dead space of the deep defect and address open venous sinuses.
The surgeon observes bleeding from a segmental artery after removing both clamps, indicating the need for further hemostatic measures.
The surgeon attempts to secure the main artery using hemo lock clips, instructing the assistant to place additional clips in the corner of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a larger MH needle to place a stitch, but decides to replace it with another stitch, requesting assistance from Brando. Hemlock clips are applied during the procedure.
The surgical team uses bolsters to manage potential bleeding while the suction device is applied to clear the surgical field.
